Business Decisions Making in Upstream Oil & Gas

Abstract— The upstream capital project oil and gas industry 
is considered a critical sector in Malaysia. Apart from its 
significant monetary contribution to the country, big data 
analysis is also applied to the supply chain operation. The 
prescriptive analysis is based on Artificial intelligence (AI), 
specifically Machine Learning (ML), which involves algorithms 
and models that enable computers to make decisions based on 
mathematical data relationships and patterns. This study aims 
to identify ML analysis in Malaysia’s upstream capital projects, 
which may improve business decisions via the use of statistical 
models and ML algorithms. Incorporating ML algorithms and 
statistical models will produce better business decision-making 
by enhancing efficiency and productivity besides fast 
monetisation and minimising risk and returns. Overall, with the 
use of mixed analysis elements, it can produce better decision 
support for stakeholders and company owners before making 
crucial business decisions. 
Keywords— Algorithm Model, Business Decisions, Upstream, 
Oil & Gas, Prescriptive Analysis 
I. INTRODUCTION 
Nowadays, many companies are involved with oil and gas 
downstream, 
upstream 
or 
integrated 
upstream 
and 
downstream activities.  Upstream activities are mainly 
focusing on exploration and production. Meanwhile, 
downstream activities focusing on activities like conversion of 
oil and gas into finished products such as petroleum, natural 
gas oils, diesel, and other energy supplies. To ensure 
business’s continuity, making the right decision is the key to 
ensure the success of a company. However, the decision-
maker cannot produce the most accurate decision without 
detailed analysis based on a huge data received. The 
incorporation of ML and big data is the best approach to 
expedite the process of decision-making.  The prescriptive 
analysis uses statistical models and ML algorithms to define 
the possibilities and to recommend actions. These models and 
algorithms can discover patterns in big data that human 
analysts may overlook. The prescriptive analysis relies on AI, 
particularly in the field of ML, which include algorithms and 
models that allow computers to make decisions based on the 
relationships and patterns of statistical data. 
II. MAIN BUSINESS OBJECTIVE 
In order to formulate strategic planning in decision-
making and maintain an efficient organisation, a strategy 
analysis is an essential step to be taken. The organisation’s 
priorities or targets are accomplished with the aid of action 
strategies. Organisations must perform daily strategy planning 
for them to progress. This activity will allow them to identify 
and evaluate critical areas that need modification, predict what 
would happen, and come with an actionable plan. It is 
important to determine how substantive progress can be 
introduced in order for an organisation to perform effectively 
[1]. 
Large, centralised organisations will stifle profitable 
operations and global economic development in the long run. 
The modern economy will remain intangible-focused to 
capitalise on the benefits of technical productivity, highly 
skilled labour, and cutting-edge imagination. The significance 
of monetisation is widely acknowledged. Systemic study in 
both logical and scientific forms is virtually non-existent. One 
of the most important properties of the degree and course of 
economic growth is the monetisation ratio, which is a 
component of the economy’s goods and services. A lot of 
these early monetisation efforts have been started based on 
recent performance. 
In today’s business paradigm, monetisation sources had 
earlier been the key to competitive advantage. This requires 
generating faster, cost-effective development plans through 
economic analysis of efficient technologies that quickly 
followed by implementing project management [2]. Each 
work must be done to ensure that the information required to 
follow the purpose. Any merging, consolidation, addition, 
analysis, cleaning, identification, packaging, access, and 
maintenance must provide the information that meets the 
requirements. This is a data management mechanism 
commonly used by many organisations, which is now used for 
monetisation purposes, as shown in Fig. 1.

IV. ATTRIBUTES WHICH INFLUENCE DECISION PATTERNS 
Functional specifications, configuration constraints, and 
consistency attribute requirements are examples of input-
driven attributes that system members prioritise based on 
market and project goals. When the platform is used in 
specific cases, functional specifications define what functions 
the technology must have to satisfy the specified and inferred 
stakeholder needs. Several factors influence decision-making, 
such as project economics, project complexity, risk, fiscal 
term, geographical, country political, and hydrocarbon price 
influence. The importance of data in revenue lies in its 
consistency and continued growth. This enables companies to 
create new business opportunities, generate more revenue, 
predict future trends, optimise current business operations, 
and generate actionable insights. 
A. Project Economics 
Prime ideas that currently drive the global innovation 
economy are different from the industrial economies of the 
past. An innovative economy alone cannot produce vitality 
and quality of life in an area if the population does not have 
the skills to participate in the growth of high-level 
employment opportunities. However, in the long run, one 
cannot maintain vitality and quality of life without being 
involved in an innovative economy.  
The success of the project refers to the solutions and a 
better strategy for implementing various processes. Success 
like that would improve the living conditions and improve 
methods of doing things. The project’s economic decisions are 
made daily from the individual level to the central banks of 
various countries [5]. Globalisation has made markets more 
interdependent and difficult to evaluate. The input model is 
primarily used in the economic assessment to forecast possible 
economic movements. Only an accurate interpretation of 
economic factors will lead to effective economic decisions. 
B. Project Complexity 
The decision of which resources will be used to achieve 
the project objectives in the most efficient way depends on the 
particular project’s characteristics, such as its objectives, 
resources, and context. There are various predictive 
techniques available, ranging from basic linear regression to 
sophisticated, efficient ones such as artificial neural networks. 
Complex models normally do well in terms of inference, but 
they are elusive and unable to clarify forecasts or patterns 
found. [6]. According to Shtub et al. [6],  complexity is the 
root of the project concept. The factors determining a project’s 
implementation include unprecedented complexity in its 
design, development, and implementation. Remington et al. 
[7] agree that complexity is vital for project management 
because of decision-making and goal achievement difficulties. 
However, they show a lack of operational definition for 
complex projects. 
C. Risk & Influence 
Risk is a necessary component of decision-making. 
Furthermore, risk perception may be seen as an individual risk 
assessment, and the accuracy of risk assessment is dependent 
on the availability of accurate risk data. Several factors 
identified as influencing risk perceptions associated with risk 
design are framing effects, trust and confidence sources, and 
target risk. A review of Kusev et al. [8] shows that decision-
making does not depend on the context. In particular, the 
difference in contextual risk descriptions, such as the amount 
of money and accessibility to clean content, make decisions 
about the events in memory and emotion are activated to 
change the selection of risks [9]. 
D. Fiscal Term 
The extractive industry expands and works in most 
countries 
through 
partnerships 
between 
sovereign 
governments and private corporations. The fiscal factors that 
control these parties’ partnership specify how the financial 
gains and costs of the extractive project can be shared. Terms 
can be found in country regulations that extend to all 
programmes or in-person contracts that regulate the website’s 
rights and responsibilities of a single project. 
Fiscal terms in the oil, gas, and mining sectors must be 
organised around four main characteristics of the extractive 
sector [10]: (i). Petroleum and mineral resources are not 
limited. Therefore the government must generate sufficient 
returns to balance the country with the value of its depleted 
assets; (ii). Extractive projects require significant initial 
investment before income begins to flow; (iii). Geological 
risks, market fluctuations, technological uncertainty, and 
political risks are all common project risks.; and (iv). 
Extractive income has the potential to represent the dominant 
portion of a country’s public income. 
E. Geographical 
Geography is the study of locations and people’s 
interactions with their surroundings. Geographers study the 
physical characteristics of the earth’s crust and the human 
civilisation that exists there. They investigate how human 
society communicates with the natural world and how their 
surroundings influence humans. For additional translation 
material, further information about this source text is needed. 
Some people struggle to grasp the full breadth of geography 
because, unlike other disciplines, geography is not defined by 
a single subject as it deals with many issues and variations 
related to culture, politics, placement, plants, landforms, and 
many more. 
F. Political climate 
In a world of conflict, examining national political stability 
perceptions is an important metric to consider prior the 
formulation of social and economic policies. Political stability 
assists world leaders, companies and organisations to 
determine the amount of investment they should pour into a 
certain country. For developed and affluent countries,  
political stability is given utmost importance as; theoretically, 
political stability may provide a more stable business climate. 
Businesses must decide whether a country trusts in free trade, 
regulatory oversight, or government interference; hence the 
investment is worthy, and the risk can be minimised.  
The country’s view on capitalism is also another factor to 
be considered in business operations. In a broad sense, 
capitalism is an economic system in which production tools 
are privately owned and operated. On the other hand, a 
centralised economy is one in which the government or state 
directs and controls the economy, including the means of 
development 
and 
decision-making 
[11]. 
Historically, 
democratic governments have favoured liberalism, while 
totalitarian regimes have favoured state-run economic 
policies.
